questions.text: A relation R in a set A is called reflexive if:
options: (a, a) ∈ R for every a ∈ A
(a, b) ∈ R and (b, c) ∈ R implies (a, c) ∈ R
questions.explanation: Reflexivity means every element is related to itself.

questions.text: A relation R in a set A is symmetric if (a, b) ∈ R implies:
questions.explanation: Symmetry means if a is related to b, then b is also related to a.

questions.text: A relation R in set A is transitive if (a, b) ∈ R and (b, c) ∈ R implies:
questions.explanation: Transitivity bridges the relation through a middle element.

questions.text: A relation which is reflexive, symmetric and transitive is called:
questions.explanation: These three properties together define an equivalence relation.

questions.text: The relation R = {(1,1), (2,2), (3,3)} on set A = {1,2,3} is:
questions.explanation: This identity relation satisfies all three properties.

questions.text: Let A = {1, 2, 3}. The number of equivalence relations containing (1, 2) is:
questions.explanation: The relations are {(1,1), (2,2), (3,3), (1,2), (2,1)} and the universal relation.

questions.text: A function f: X → Y is one-one (injective) if:
options: Every y in Y has an image
questions.explanation: Injective functions map distinct elements to distinct elements.

questions.text: A function f: X → Y is onto (surjective) if:
options: Every x has a unique image
questions.explanation: Surjective means every element in the codomain is an image of at least one element in the domain.

questions.text: A function which is both one-one and onto is called:
questions.explanation: Bijections are perfectly paired mappings between two sets.

questions.text: f: R → R defined by f(x) = x² is:
questions.explanation: f(1)=f(-1)=1 (not 1-1) and negative numbers have no pre-image (not onto).

questions.text: f: N → N defined by f(x) = x² is:
questions.explanation: It is one-one for natural numbers, but 2, 3 etc. are not squares of any natural number.

questions.text: The total number of relations from a set A with n elements to itself is:
questions.explanation: A relation is a subset of A × A, which has n² elements.

questions.text: The smallest equivalence relation on set A is:
questions.explanation: The identity relation {(a,a) : a ∈ A} is the minimal set to satisfy reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity.

questions.text: The greatest integer function f(x) = [x] from R to R is:
questions.explanation: f(1.2)=f(1.5)=1 (not 1-1) and non-integers are not in the range (not onto).

questions.text: If f: A → B and g: B → C are one-one, then gof: A → C is:
questions.explanation: The composition of two injective functions is always injective.

questions.text: Let R be a relation on N defined by x + 2y = 8. The domain of R is:
questions.explanation: Possible pairs (x,y) in N: (6,1), (4,2), (2,3). Domain is {2, 4, 6}.

questions.text: The number of reflexive relations on a set with 3 elements is:
questions.explanation: Formula is 2^(n²-n). For n=3, 2^(9-3) = 2^6 = 64.

questions.text: If f(x) = |x|, then f is:
questions.explanation: f(x) = |x| is the standard modulus function.

questions.text: Every identity relation is an equivalence relation.
questions.explanation: Identity relations are reflexive, symmetric, and transitive by definition.

questions.text: A function f: A → B is bijective if and only if it has a/an:
questions.explanation: Bijectivity is the necessary and sufficient condition for the existence of an inverse function.

questions.text: The empty relation on a non-empty set is symmetric and transitive.
questions.explanation: Symmetry and transitivity are vacuously true for an empty set of pairs.

questions.text: Let f: R → R be given by f(x) = 3x. Then f is:
questions.explanation: Linear functions with non-zero slope are bijections over R.

questions.text: The range of the function f(x) = sin x is:
questions.explanation: The sine function oscillates between -1 and 1, inclusive.

questions.text: If n(A) = 3, then the number of one-one functions from A to itself is:
questions.explanation: Number of bijections on a set of size n is n!. 3! = 6.

questions.text: An equivalence relation partitions a set into disjoint:
questions.explanation: The union of all equivalence classes is the set itself, and classes are mutually exclusive.

questions.text: The intersection of two equivalence relations on a set is:
questions.explanation: If R and S are equivalence relations, R ∩ S is also reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.

questions.text: Which of the following is an injective but not surjective function from N to N?
questions.explanation: In N, x+1 never equals 1, and 2x never equals odd numbers.

questions.text: A relation R = {(a,b) : a is brother of b} on a set of girls is:
questions.explanation: In a set of girls, no one can be a 'brother'.

questions.text: The number of surjective functions from {1, 2, ..., n} to {a, b} is:
questions.explanation: Total functions (2^n) minus the two constant functions which are not onto.

questions.text: Signum function f: R → R is defined as f(x) = 1 if x>0, 0 if x=0, -1 if x<0. Its range is:
questions.explanation: The signum function outputs only three discrete values.

questions.text: If R is symmetric, then R inverse (R⁻¹) is:
questions.explanation: By definition of symmetry, R = R⁻¹.

questions.text: Let A = {1, 2, 3}. The relation R = {(1,2), (2,3)} is:
questions.explanation: It lacks (1,3) which is required for transitivity.

questions.text: Maximum number of equivalence relations on a set with 3 elements is:
questions.explanation: Calculated by Bell numbers: B3 = 5.

questions.text: Function f(x) = x + cos x is one-one over R.
questions.explanation: f'(x) = 1 - sin x ≥ 0. Since it's monotonically increasing, it is one-one.

questions.text: Number of binary operations on a set with n elements is:
questions.explanation: A binary operation is a function from A × A to A.

questions.text: The set of all first elements of the ordered pairs in a relation R is called:
questions.explanation: Domain refers to the set of 'inputs' used in the relation.

questions.text: If a relation is 'is perpendicular to' on a set of lines, it is:
questions.explanation: If L1 ⊥ L2, then L2 ⊥ L1. But L1 is not ⊥ to itself, and transitivity fails (L1 || L3).

questions.text: The number of functions from set A (size m) to set B (size n) is:
questions.explanation: Each of the m elements in A has n choices in B.

questions.text: Constant function f(x) = k is:
questions.explanation: All elements map to the same single value k.

questions.text: Inverse of a bijection is always a bijection.
questions.explanation: The inverse function undoes the mapping while preserving the 1-1 and onto properties.
